coredata.dc:title: A reversible fluorescence nanoswitch based on carbon quantum dots nanoassembly for detection of pyrophosphate ion
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Chai, Lu Jing
Zhou, Jin
Feng, Hui
Lin, Jing Jing
Qian, Zhao Sheng
coredata.dc:description: A convenient and reversible nanoswitch with the assistance of cerium ions based on aggregation and disaggregation of carbon quantum dots was developed to achieve highly sensitive detection of pyrophosphate ions. Two states including aggregation state and disaggregation state corresponding to fluorescence on and off signaling can be readily switched in a reversible way. The aggregation state is resulted from large nanoassembly formed by carbon quantum dots and cerium ions due to their strong complexation affinity. The disaggregation state comes from competitive combination between cerium ion and pyrophosphate ion. The PPi ion severs as the external chemical stimulus to CQDs/Ce(III) nanoassembly, and its quantity is responsive to fluorescence intensity of the sensing system. Quantitative evaluation of PPi in a broad range from 0.3 to 29.7μM with the detection limit of 0.1μM can be realized in this way. The PPi detection in human HeLa cells was also assessed, and fluorescence switching between ON and OFF can be readily observed in response to presence and absence of PPi.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Pyrophosphate ion
Fluorescence nanoswitch
Nanoassembly

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Quantitative determination of PPi based on CQDs
Selectivity of the sensing system toward PPi
Cytotoxicity test to human HeLa cells
Bioimaging for PPi in human HeLa cells
Construction and assessment of the nanoswitch based on CQDs
Assessment of quantitative detection for PPi based on CQDs and Ce(III)

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We are thankful for the support by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21405142 , 21005073 and 21275131 ) and Zhejiang Province (No. LY13B050001 , LQ13B050002 and ZJHX201403 ). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of carbon quantum dots-based nanoswitch shifting between aggregation state and disaggregation state in response to Ce(III) and PPi ion respectively.

(A) TEM images of original CQDs. Inset: High-resolution TEM. (B) Nanoassembly of CQDs and cerium ions. (C) High-resolution TEM of the nanoassemby. (D) Re-dispersed CQDs with cerium ions in the presence of PPi.

The fluorescence spectra of CQDs, CQDs in the presence of Ce(III), and in the presence of Ce(III) and PPi. Inset: fluorescence photographs of the fluorescence nanoswitch in ON and OFF state in response to PPi and Ce(III).

(A) The fluorescence spectra of the mixture containing CQDs solution and Ce(III) with different concentrations from 0.0 to 84.2μM. (B) The fluorescence spectra of the mixture containing CQDs, Ce(III) and PPi with different concentrations from 0.0 to 69.3μM. (C) The fitting curve between fluorescence intensity and concentration of added PPi. (D) The selectivity of the sensing system toward to different kinds of anions and some phosphate-containing species including F−, Cl−, Br−, I−, NO3 −, SO4 2−, HCO3 −, AC−, Pi, NADH, DNA, ATP and PPi. The concentration of Ce(III) is 200.0μM and the concentration of each chosen species is 83.3μM. Pi, NADH, ATP and PPi represent phosphate ion, nicotiamide adenine dinucleotide, adenosine triphosphate and pyrophosphate ion respectively.

Confocal fluorescence images in human Hela cells. A1–A3: mere cells; B1–B3: cells incubated with CQDs for 20h; C1–C3: cells incubated with CQDs/Ce(III) mixture for 0.5h, where the concentration of Ce(III) is 100.0μM; D1–D3: Cells incubated with CQDs/Ce(III) mixture for 0.5h, and then incubated with K4P2O7 (100.0μM) for 0.5h. Numbers 1, 2 and 3 represent bright field, fluorescence and overlap field respectively. Emission was collected at the range from 500 to 600nm upon excitation at 488nm.

head.ce:title: A reversible fluorescence nanoswitch based on carbon quantum dots nanoassembly for detection of pyrophosphate ion

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Life Science, Zhejiang Normal University, Jinhua 321004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Life Science, Zhejiang Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Beijing National Laboratory for Molecular Sciences, Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Living Biosystems, Institute of Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100190, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Beijing National Laboratory for Molecular Sciences, Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Living Biosystems, Institute of Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A reversible fluorescence nanoswitch by integrating carbon quantum dots/Ce(III) nanoassembly and pyrophosphate ion was developed. This nanoswitch can serve as a highly sensitive sensing platform for PPi detection, and operate well inside human HeLa cells.

A convenient and reversible nanoswitch with the assistance of cerium ions based on aggregation and disaggregation of carbon quantum dots was developed to achieve highly sensitive detection of pyrophosphate ions. Two states including aggregation state and disaggregation state corresponding to fluorescence on and off signaling can be readily switched in a reversible way. The aggregation state is resulted from large nanoassembly formed by carbon quantum dots and cerium ions due to their strong complexation affinity. The disaggregation state comes from competitive combination between cerium ion and pyrophosphate ion. The PPi ion severs as the external chemical stimulus to CQDs/Ce(III) nanoassembly, and its quantity is responsive to fluorescence intensity of the sensing system. Quantitative evaluation of PPi in a broad range from 0.3 to 29.7μM with the detection limit of 0.1μM can be realized in this way. The PPi detection in human HeLa cells was also assessed, and fluorescence switching between ON and OFF can be readily observed in response to presence and absence of PPi.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, considerable effort has been devoted to the design and fabrication of artificial nanoswitches/machines with various molecules for diverse applications [1–6]. Molecular nanoswitches are a diverse class of construct capable of changing photochemical stimuli to perform a range of useful tasks from species sensing to reaction control. The majority of the preceding work focuses on construction of nanoswitches based on molecular DNA through multiple hydrogen bonding interaction [6–8], and these DNA-based nanoswitches were used to achieve pH monitoring, DNA/RNA recognition and DNAzyme activity control [9–11]. Except based on multiple hydrogen bonding between DNA, another variety of nanoswitches on the basis of coordination between metal ions and molecules were also developed to control catalytic reaction recently [12–14]. However, quite few such nanoswitches have been reported up to date.

As the introduction of nanomaterials into diverse areas, they have been exploited to build DNA-connected nanoswitches as a basic block. The nanoswitch constructed by DNA-based magnetic nanoparticle assembly is capable of screening of DNA-cleaving agents [15]. A pH-driven DNA nanoswitch employed gold nanoparticles and mesoporous silica to realize responsive controlled release [16]. Inspired by the two preceding reports and mediation of coordination of metal ions, herein we demonstrated a fluorescence nanoswitch based on carbon quantum dots (CQDs) nanoassembly and pyrophosphate ions (PPi) for the first time, where this nanoswitch is capable of changing its physical state from aggregated state and dispersed state accompanying its fluorescence OFF and ON as signaling readout after it is triggered by PPi. In this sense, this construct can be regarded as a nanoswitch based on CQDs. CQDs as a novel promising fluorescent nanomaterial have drawn much attention from a variety of fields including bioimaging, biosensor and photocatalysis [17–20]. It has been reported that CQDs not only show strong fluorescence and good biocompatibility, but also can be used to label human cells and synthesize varieties of probes for detection of chemicals and biological species [21–31]. In addition, it is reported that pyrophosphate anion (PPi) not only involves in DNA polymerization [32], enzymatic reactions [33], and cellular metabolism [34], but also are related to some severe medical conditions in term of their levels [35]. Early efforts have suggested that PPi could be used as a potential biomarker for the clinic diagnosis and therapy of arthritic disease [36]. Thus, the exploration of convenient, reliable and sensitive detection methods for PPi is also an interesting task.

In this work, we report a reversible fluorescence nanoswitch based on carbon quantum dots nanoassembly in response to pyrophosphate ions. This nanoswitch changes its states between aggregation and disaggregation in response to cerium(III) ion and pyrophosphate ions respectively in a reversible way, accompanying the signal readout of fluorescence ON/OFF. This nanoswitch is easily achieved by taking advantage of the following unique designed elements: (1) CQDs as the key signaling element possess strong green fluorescence in its dispersion state in aqueous solution; (2) abundance of carboxyl groups on the surface of CQDs endows them to aggregate with the assistance of Ce(III) ion because carboxyl group is a good complexation ligand to Ce(III); (3) precipitation reaction between PPi and Ce(III) enables disaggregation of CQDs nanoassembly. Scheme 1 shows the schematic illustration of CQDs-based nanoswitch shifting between aggregation state and disaggregation state in response to Ce(III) and PPi respectively. Due to strong complexation ability of cerium ions to carboxylate groups on the surface of CQDs, the presence of cerium ions triggers severe aggregation of CQDs, which is denoted as State I. At State I, severe aggregation of CQDs results in their fluorescence quenching while its fluorescence signal is at switch-off state. Because of stronger affinity of PPi to cerium ions than carboxylate, PPi will grab cerium ions from CQDs-Ce aggregates to from preferential Ce-PPi precipitate. The introduction of PPi into CQDs nanoassembly initiates the disaggregation and finally re-disperses CQDs in aqueous solution, which is denoted as State II, the fluorescence switches on accompanying the formation of Ce-PPi precipitate. The generated precipitate can be easily removed by centrifugation to reach initial mere CQDs solution, and then the switch can operate in a recyclable way. Otherwise, this nanoswitch can also operate in a reversible way without further separation of generated precipitate. On the basis of the fluorescence nanoswitch, highly selective and sensitive detection of PPi in vitro was readily accomplished, and intracellular detection of PPi in living cells was also assessed.

Triple-distilled water was used throughout the experimental process. Activated carbon, Cerium (III) nitrate hexahydrate (Ce(NO3)3·6H2O), pyrophosphate (PPi), tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane and other salts were purchased from Aladdin Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane buffer solution (Tris–HCl, pH=7.4) was prepared by mixing stock solutions of Tris and HCl. All reagents were of analytical grade and without any further purification.

The synthesis of carbon quantum dots is modified according to previous papers [37,38]. Activated carbon (2.0g) was added into a mixture of concentrated sulfuric acid (180ml) and nitric acid (60ml). The mixture was heated at 80°C for 5h. The mixture was cooled and diluted with deionized water (800ml). The dark CQDs solution was neutralized with sodium hydroxide. The final product solution was dialyzed in a dialysis bag (1000Da) for 3 days, and then the resultant solution was further treated with the dialysis bag (50,000Da) to remove large non-fluorescent materials. The outside solution of the bag was collected and concentrated for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative determination of PPi based on CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: For optimization of incubation time for fluorescence quenching by cerium ions, 15.0μL of cerium(III) solution (10.0mM) was added into 3.0mL of CQDs (Tris–HCl, pH=7.4) solution. Then, the fluorescence of mixed solution was recorded after different shaking time of 0.5,1, 2, 3, 4, and 5min respectively. Under the optimum incubation time, fluorescence quenching of CQDs was assessed with continuous addition of cerium ions. The fluorescence intensity of the mixtures containing CQDs and varying amounts of Ce(III) was monitored using fluorescence spectrometer at the optimal excitation wavelength. For optimization of incubation time for fluorescence recovery by PPi, the fluorescence of CQDs was first quenched by adding 15.0μL of cerium(III) solution (10.0mM) into 3.0mL of CQDs (Tris–HCl, pH=7.4) solution. Then, 25.0μL of PPi solution (10.0mM) was introduced to the above mixture. The fluorescence was recorded after different shaking time of 0.5, 1, 1.5, 2, 2.5, and 3min respectively.

For quantitative determination of PPi, 20.0μL of cerium(III) ions (10.0mM) was first added into 3.0mL of CQDs solution to quench the fluorescence. A certain different concentrations of PPi were added into the above mixture respectively. After 2min incubation time, the fluorescence spectra were recorded at the excitation wavelength of 452nm. The concentrations of PPi were 1.65, 3.3, 6.6, 16.5, 23.1, 29.7, 42.9, 56.1 and 69.3μM respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of the sensing system toward PPi
ce:section.ce:para: Different kinds of anions and some phosphate-containing species including F−, Cl−, Br−, I−, NO3 −, SO4 2−, HCO3 −, AC−, Pi, NADH, DNA, ATP and PPi were selected to evaluate the influence of anions on the fluorescence of CQDs-Ce system and assess the selectivity toward PPi. A certain amount of cerium ions were added into 3.00ml of CQDs solution, where the concentration of cerium ions was 50.0μM. A certain amount of each anion was then introduced into the above CQDs-Ce system, and then the fluorescence spectra of resulting mixtures were monitored at the excitation wavelength of 452nm. For all the experiments, the concentration of the chosen species was 83.3μM, and the Tris–HCl buffer (pH 7.4) was selected as the buffer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity test to human HeLa cells
ce:section.ce:para: The cytotoxicity of CQDs and CQDs-Ce(III) nanoassembly on human HeLa cells was evaluated by the standard MTT assay. Briefly, HeLa cells were seeded in 96-well U-bottom plates at a density of 7000cells/well, and incubated with CQDs and CQDs-Ce(III) nanoassembly, the concentration of Ce(III) varies from 0 to 200μM corresponding to the amount of CQDs ranging from 0 to 200μg/mL. Then, the culture media were discarded, and 0.1mL of the MTT solution (0.5mgmL−1 in DMEM) was added to each well, followed by incubation at 37°C for 4h. The supernatant was abandoned, and 110μL of DMSO was added to each well to dissolve the formed formazan. After shaking the plates for 10min, absorbance values of the wells were read with a microplate reader at 490nm. The cell viability rate (VR) was calculated according to the equation: VR= A/A 0 ×100%, where A is the absorbance of the experimental group (i.e., the cells were treated by CQDs and CQDs-Ce(III) nanoassembly respectively) and A0 is the absorbance of the control group (i.e., the cells were untreated by CQDs and CQDs-Ce(III) nanoassembly). The cell survival rate from the control group was considered to be 100%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bioimaging for PPi in human HeLa cells
ce:section.ce:para: HeLa cells used in this study were cultured in DMEM containing 10% (v/v) fetal bovine serum and 1% (v/v) penicillin-streptomycin at 37°C in a 5% CO2 incubator. For fluorescence imaging, the adherent cells grown on glass-bottom culture dishes (Mat Tek Co.) containing 1mL of culture medium were first incubated with CQDs for 20h at 37°C, and then washed with PBS solution (pH 7.4). After co-culturing, a dish containing the cells then incubated with a PBS solution of Ce(NO3)3 (100.0μM) for 0.5h at 37°C. Another dish containing above system was washed with PBS, then the cells were incubated with a PBS solution of PPi (100.0μM) for 0.5h at 37°C. The stained cells were washed three times with PBS buffer. Then the treated cells were imaged by fluorescence microscopy.

The morphologies of all samples were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), which was performed on a JEOL-2100F instrument with an accelerating voltage of 200kV. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy analyses were conducted using a Kratos Axis ULTRA X-ray photoelectron spectrometer with a 165nm hemisphereical electron energy analyzer. The UV–vis spectra were recorded on a Perkin Elmer Lambda 950 spectrometer. The photoluminescence spectra were conducted on a PerkinElmer LS-55 fluorescence spectrometer, and lifetimes were determined using a FLS920 fluorescence spectrophotometer. Fluorescence experiments were performed on an FV 1000-IX81 confocal laser scanning microscope (Olympus, Japan) with FV5-LAMAR for excitation at 488nm and a variable bandpass emission filter set to 500–600nm through a 100×1.4 NA objective.

The preparation of CQDs was modified according to the procedure in the previous papers [37,38]. In the preceding preparation, dilute nitric acid from 1 to 5M was used to oxidize activated carbon which consumed quite long time under the refluxed condition from 12 to 24hours. To further reduce the reaction time, concentrated sulfuric and nitric acid was utilized to achieve a high yield of nearly 50%, by which the reaction time can be as short as 5hours. Moreover, as-prepared CQDs possess different fluorescence color from those based on the preceding methods. The as-prepared CQDs show strong yellowish green fluorescence, and are highly soluble in water due to abundance in oxygen-containing groups on the surface of CQDs. A vast number of well-dispersed CQDs were observed from TEM image in Fig. 1A, and clear crystalline structure of CQDs was also displayed in HR-TEM image. A relatively narrow size distribution ranging from 2 to 4nm for CQDs was present in Fig. S1. XPS spectra of CQDs were recorded (Fig. S2) to analyze the composition of CQDs. Two distinct peaks at 284.8 and 288.6eV were displayed in the C1s spectrum, which are originated from carbon atoms of CC, CO and CO, respectively. Elemental analysis of CQDs indicates that the CQDs mainly consist of oxygen (25.5at%) and carbon (74.5at%). These results illustrated that a vast number of oxygen-containing groups including hydroxyl and carboxyl functionals were generated on the surface of CQDs after chemical oxidation, which greatly contributes to excellent water solubility of as-prepared CQDs. Strong yellowish green fluorescence of CQDs can be observed under the UV lamp, and fluorescence spectra in Fig. S3 illustrated that CQDs solution can fluoresce with an emission peak at 518nm at the optimum excitation wavelength of 452nm. The emission spectra of CQDs at different excitation wavelengths shows that the peak of emission spectra is nearly not shifted as the change of the excitation wavelength, which suggests good monodispersion of CQDs in size and fluorescence, and provides an advantage over other carbon dots with shifting emission for analytical application. The quantum yield of CQDs was determined as 0.022 using rhodamine 6G as standard reference, and the lifetime was calculated to be 6.2ns, which shows similar photoluminescence property to CQDs reported previously [21–24].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction and assessment of the nanoswitch based on CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Since it has been reported that the fluorescence of CQDs is responsive to some metal ions such as Fe(III), and furthermore we anticipate that some metal cations could complex with CQDs through their surface carboxyl groups, thus we evaluated the influence of fluorescence of as-prepared CQDs by common metal cations including Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Al3+, Ag+, Zn2+, Fe3+, Ni2+, Co2+, Pb2+, Fe2+, Cu2+ and Ce(III). As shown in Fig. S4, among these metal ions, six metal ions including Ni2+, Co2+, Pb2+, Fe2+, Cu2+ and Ce(III) show apparent impact on the fluorescence of CQDs with a quenching effect, and Ce(III) shows the strongest quenching ability to the fluorescence. The presence of Ce(III) (66.0μM) leads to nearly complete fluorescence quenching of CQDs as displayed in Fig. 2 . It was reported that cerium ion can be used to aggregate molecular indicators to quenching the fluorescence through strong coordination between carboxyl group and cerium ion [39], and thus it is speculated that strong complexation between CQDs and cerium ion leads to severe aggregation of CQDs, resulting in fluorescence quenching. To verify the quenching mechanism by Ce(III), we performed the TEM of the mixture containing CQDs and Ce(III) as shown in Fig. 1B and C. It is found that a great deal of large aggregates composing of CQDs and Ce(III) up to 200nm in diameter formed, and high-resolution TEM clearly displayed the nanoassembly consists of a large number of CQDs in a stacking way. Element mapping of Ce(III) for large CQDs-Ce(III) aggregate was also conducted as shown in Fig. S5, which clearly demonstrated that cerium ions were homogenously distributed in the aggregates. Thus, it is reasonable to suppose that the formation of nanoassembly mainly contributes to the fluorescence quenching of CQDs in the presence of Ce(III). The nanoassembly can be regarded as FL OFF state. After the introduction of PPi anion with the same concentration to added Ce(III) into nanoassembly solution, we found that the fluorescence intensity of the mixture was largely restored up to 90% of the original value. As the rising of PPi in concentration up to twice amount of Ce(III), the fluorescence intensity can be totally recovered. The TEM in Fig. 1D indicates that the formed nanoassemblies have decomposed to dispersed CQDs in the presence of PPi, which can be attributed to precipitation reaction between PPi and Ce(III). Hence the recovered fluorescence comes from re-dispersed CQDs in solution, which can be regarded as FL ON state. Therefore, the nanoswitch with FL OFF state and ON state corresponding to aggregation state and disaggregation state can be achieved by integrating CQDs, Ce(III) and PPi. The presence of Ce(III) switches off the fluorescence, whereas PPi anions trigger switch-on of the fluorescence. More importantly, the constructed nanoswitch is reversible shifting between ON and OFF states by introducing PPi or Ce(III) ion into CQDs solution. The formed precipitate Ce-PPi can be readily removed from the solution by centrifugation to re-generate CQDs solution for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessment of quantitative detection for PPi based on CQDs and Ce(III)
ce:section.ce:para: We further evaluated the feasibility of CQDs/Ce(III) nanoassembly as a fluorescent sensing platform for PPi detection. The condition optimization including the added amount of cerium(III) ions, and the incubation time for fluorescence quenching and enhancement were first carried out. Fig. 3A shows gradual decrease in fluorescence intensity with the increase of cerium ion in amount, and the fluorescence intensity reaches the minimum when the concentration of cerium ions is up to 84.2μM. Fig. S6 displays the changes in fluorescence intensity of the systems as lengthening incubation time for quenching and recovery experiments. Both experiments show that the fluorescence intensity remains stable when the incubation time is over 2min, indicating the fast response time to detect PPi. By compromising the above results, the concentration of 66.7μM for cerium ion and 2min incubation time were chosen as the optimal conditions for the following quantitative determination of PPi.

Under the optimal conditions, the sensing performance of this system for detection of PPi was evaluated by adding different concentrations of target into the preceding CQDs/Ce(III) nanoassembly. The fluorescence response of these systems was recorded by introducing varied concentrations of PPi in pH 7.4 buffer solutions at room temperature. Fig. 3B indicates that the fluorescence intensity of the system could be gradually restored upon the addition of different concentrations of PPi. The fluorescence intensity of the system keeps nearly unchanged when the concentration of added PPi is over 69.3μM. A good linear fitting curve between fluorescence intensity and concentration of PPi can be obtained in the range of 0.3–29.7μM (Fig. 3 C). The linear regression equation can be expressed as y =5.56x +200.86 where R 2 =0.994. The detection limit for PPi was calculated to be 0.1μM as estimated from the derived calibration curve (≥3 standard deviations). The sensitivity of our method is comparable to or slightly better than those based on gold nanoparticles with the detection limit ranging from 0.08 to 0.2μM [40–42], and that based on functionalized silica with the detection limit of 0.1μM [43]. To investigate the selectivity of our method, different kinds of anions and some phosphate-containing species that were considered to possibly interfere with PPi sensing were added into CQDs/Ce(III) nanoassembly system, and then the fluorescence response was recorded. Fig. 3D shows separate addition of each kind of species including F−, Cl−, Br−, I−, SO4 2−, NO3 −, HCO3 −, Ac−, NADH and DNA into CQDs/Ce(III) nanoassembly system does not result in an obvious change in fluorescence intensity while different degrees of apparent fluorescence enhancement can be observed after the addition of ATP and PPi. However, in comparison with the sharp increase of fluorescence intensity by PPi, the influence of ATP can be considered to be acceptable because the selectivity value (I−I 0)/I 0 for PPi is up to 6 while the value for ATP is less than 2. These results substantially suggest that the selectivity of our method for PPi detection performs well in the presence of these potentially interfering species except ATP.

It has been reported that pyrophosphate ion (PPi) is widely present in the cell and plasma of plants and mammalian at the micromolar level, and thus its mapping at cellular level is of great significance to explore its function in cell and related tissues [44,45]. In order to explore the potential application in detection and mapping of PPi at cellular level, we first assessed the cytotoxicity of CQDs-Ce(III) nanoassembly and then applied this nanoswitch to detect PPi in human HeLa cells using confocal fluorescence image. The results on cytotoxicity assessment shown in Fig. S7 indicated that mere CQDs possess low toxicity to human HeLa cells because total cell viability remains at the high level even when the concentration of CQDs is 100μg/mL, suggesting as-prepared CQDs have similar biocompatibility to those reported previously [46,47]. The total cell viability of human HeLa cells incubated with CQDs-Ce(III) mixture retains as high as more than 90% when the concentration of CQDs and Ce(III) in the mixture is up to 100μg/mL and 100μM respectively, and no apparent enhancement of CQDs-Ce(III) mixture in toxicity can be observed with comparison to mere CQDs. Therefore, it can be concluded that CQDs-Ce(III) mixture possesses low toxicity to human HeLa cells, and can be used for potential application in cells. As illustrated in Fig. 4 , we first recorded the fluorescence images of mere HeLa cells with collected emission ranging from 500 to 600nm at excitation of 488nm, and no fluorescence can be observed. After incubated with CQDs solution for 20hours, HeLa cells showed bright green fluorescence in the cytoplasm part. However, when cerium ions were introduced into the incubation solution containing CQDs, bright fluorescence in cells after such treatment disappeared, fluorescence switching off by cerium ions. The presence of PPi triggering the transition of the state of the nanoswitch makes HeLa cells fluoresce again. From the fluorescence images in Fig. 4, we can see that the nanoswitch composing of CQDs, Ce(III) and PPi operates very well inside cells, which not only endows the detection of PPi in cells but also provides a potential application that this nanoswitch is capable for controllable delivery and release of certain target using external stimulus of PPi.

In summary, a novel fluorescent nanoswitch on the basis of carbon quantum dots through integrating competitive interaction among CQDs, Ce(III) and PPi was designed. Two states including aggregation state and disaggregation state corresponding to fluorescence off and on signaling can be readily switched in a reversible way. The aggregation state is resulted from large nanoassembly formed by carbon quantum dots and cerium ions due to their strong complexation affinity. The disaggregation state comes from competitive combination between cerium ion and pyrophosphate ion. The PPi ion severs as the external chemical stimulus to CQDs/Ce(III) nanoassembly, and its quantity is responsive to fluorescence intensity of the sensing system. This new fluorescence nanoswitch is expected to realize controllable delivery and release of drugs in vivo in the future.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are thankful for the support by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21405142, 21005073 and 21275131) and Zhejiang Province (No. LY13B050001, LQ13B050002 and ZJHX201403).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2015.05.070.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are the supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A DNA nanomachine that maps spatial and temporal pH changes inside living cells
An autonomous DNA nanomachine maps spatiotemporal pH changes in a multicellular living organism
Light-driven DNA nanomachine with a photoresponsive molecular engine
A twisting electronic nanoswitch made of DNA
A DNA nanomachine based on a duplex–triplex transition
Fabricating a reversible and regenerable raman-active substrate with a biomolecule-controlled DNA nanomachine
A reversible pH-driven DNA nanoswitch array
An on-demand four-way junction DNAzyme nanoswitch driven by inosine-based partial strand displacement
A reversible nanoswitch as an ON–OFF photocatalyst
Mitochondria-targeted cancer therapy using a light-up probe with aggregation-induced-emission characteristics
Reversible ON/OFF nanoswitch for organocatalysis: mimicking the locking and unlocking operation of CaMKII
DNA-based magnetic nanoparticle assembly acts as a magnetic relaxation nanoswitch allowing screening of DNA-cleaving agents
A pH-driven DNA nanoswitch for responsive controlled release
Control the size and surface chemistry of graphene for the rising fluorescent materials
Graphene quantum dots: emergent nanolights for bioimaging, sensors, catalysis and photovoltaic devices
Focusing on luminescent graphene quantum dots: current status and future perspectives
Si-doped carbon quantum dots: a facile and general preparation strategy, bioimaging application, and multifunctional sensor
DNA nanosensor based on biocompatible graphene quantum dots and carbon nanotubes
A universal fluorescence sensing strategy based on biocompatible graphene quantum dots and graphene oxide for the detection of DNA
Dual-colored graphene quantum dots-labeled nanoprobes/graphene oxide: functional carbon materials for respective and simultaneous detection of DNA and thrombin

Green, low-cost synthesis of photoluminescent carbon dots by hydrothermal treatment of willow bark and their application as an effective photocatalyst for fabricating Au nanoparticles-reduced graphene oxide nanocomposites for glucose detection
Economical, Green synthesis of fluorescent carbon nanoparticles and their use as probes for sensitive and selective detection of mercury(ii) ions

Hydrothermal treatment of grass: a low-cost, green route to nitrogen-doped, carbon-rich, photoluminescent polymer nanodots as an effective fluorescent sensing platform for label-free detection of Cu(II) ions

A general strategy for the production of photoluminescent carbon nitride dots from organic amines and their application as novel peroxidase-like catalysts for colorimetric detection of H2O2 and glucose
An Aqueous route to multicolor photoluminescent carbon dots using silica spheres as carriers
Optical fiber sensor for Hg(II) based on carbon dots
Real-time DNA sequencing using detection of pyrophosphate release
Applying rapid genome sequencing technologies to characterize pathogen genomes
A possible role for pyrophosphate in the coordination of cytosolic and plastidial carbon metabolism within the potato tuber
Association between synovial fluid levels of inorganic pyrophosphate and short term radiographic outcome of knee osteoarthritis
Commercially activated carbon as the source for producing multicolor photoluminescent carbon dots by chemcial oxidation
Extraction of electrochmiluminescent oxidized carbon quantum dots from activated carbon
Coordination ligand exchange of a xanthene probe-Ce(III) complex for selective fluorescence sensing of inorganic pyrophosphate
A highly sensitive gold nanoparticle-based colorimetric probe for pyrophosphate using a competition assay approach
Competitive coordination of Cu2+ between cysteine and pyrophosphate ion: toward sensitive and selective sensing of pyrophosphate ion in synovial fluid of arthritis patients
BSA-protected gold nanoclusters as fluorescent sensor for selective and sensitive detection of pyrophosphate
Chromo-fluorogenic sensing of pyrophosphate in aqueous media using silica functionalised with binding and reactive units
Reduced plasma pyrophosphate levels in hemodialysis patients
Signalling properties of inorganic polyphosphate in the mamalian brian
Surface functionalization of graphene quantum dots with small organic molecules from photoluminescence modulation to bioimaging applications: an experimental and theoretical investigation
Strong green-photoluminescent graphene quantum dots for bioimaging applications

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Lu Jing Chai studied at Zhejiang Normal University, and obtained her BSc in chemistry in 2012. She joined Professor Qian's research group at Zhejiang Normal University after her graduation. Her research focus on the fluorescent chemosensors and biosensors based on graphene quantum dots.

Jin Zhou Majored in chemistry at Zhejiang Normal University, and received his Master degree in analytical chemistry in 2013. He is studying at Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Living Biosystems of Chinese Academy of Sciences. His research interests focus on the development of cell imaging and fluorescent biosensors.

Hui Feng Majored in chemistry at Shandong Normal University, and received her Master degree in physical chemistry in 2006. She moved on Nanjing University, and received her PhD in 2009. She works at Zhejiang Normal University as an associate professor. Her research interests focus on the development of novel biosensors targeting at DNA, proteins and peptides.
Jing Jing Lin is studying at Zhejing Normal University for her BSc in chemistry. Her research focus on the fluorescent chemosensors and biosensors based on graphene quantum dots.

Zhao Sheng Qian studied chemistry in Nanjing University, China, and received his PhD in 2009. He started his academic career at Zhejiang Normal University, and promoted as Associate Professor in 2011. He engages in novel fluorescent sensors with carbon-based materials, and has published more than 40 scientific papers.
